### Post by Azyx on 2011-12-08
Have a Neatgear wgr614 v 5 router. I aso have a couple of klient/server-computers with mostly samba-shares.

Before i have used the DNS from ISP. Then my Ubuntu and IMac client had my roter as DNS-server.

Azyx@Intel:~$ nano /etc/resolv.conf
# Generated by NetworkManager
nameserver 192.168.0.1

I hade sometimes quite poor prefomance in finding places and many times the Web-browser wasn'tt able to find the pages. When I in the router, change from 'Get Automatically From ISP' to 'Use these DNS Servers' and I added openDNSs IP#, and also my client get this IP#, and not as before the Netgeat-router (192.168.0.1).

Now when i read resolv.conf in my clients i get:  

Azyx@Intel:~$ nano /etc/resolv.conf
# Generated by NetworkManager
nameserver 208.67.222.222
nameserver 208.67.220.220

As i understand it, so did my router before work as a DNS-server, but now It's only forward a global DNS-server there my privat network don't are.

How solve this? I can't find any thing in the Neatget router, so It work as a local DNS.

By the way, my Desktop Viewer, can still see my Computers, but I cannot browse network from Nautilus, but connect true IP#

Do i need to create a local DNS-server and can i do that on all machines or must I allway have one DNS-server on (As the Netgear-routern.

### Post by Grenage on 2012-01-18
> **Azyx said:**
> Before i have used the DNS from ISP. Then my Ubuntu and IMac client had my roter as DNS-server.

# Generated by NetworkManager
nameserver 192.168.0.

That's how most home users will run things, and it's normally ok unless the ISP DNS servers are awful, or the router has issues.

> **Azyx said:**
> I hade sometimes quite poor prefomance in finding places and many times the Web-browser wasn'tt able to find the pages. When I in the router, change from 'Get Automatically From ISP' to 'Use these DNS Servers' and I added openDNSs IP#, and also my client get this IP#, and not as before the Netgeat-router (192.168.0.1).

Azyx@Intel:~$ nano /etc/resolv.conf
# Generated by NetworkManager
nameserver 208.67.222.222
nameserver 208.67.220.220

You can change the DNS servers locally, on the clients, or you can change the DNS servers on the router (if it supports forwarding).  Both are options.

> **Azyx said:**
> As i understand it, so did my router before work as a DNS-server, but now It's only forward a global DNS-server there my privat network don't are.

How solve this? I can't find any thing in the Neatget router, so It work as a local DNS.

By the way, my Desktop Viewer, can still see my Computers, but I cannot browse network from Nautilus, but connect true IP#

Unless you're using the router as your DNS server (which forwards any fresh queries), your internal devices won't be aware of the other internal devices by name.  That's because the external DNS server doesn't know about your clients.

Generally you would either configure an alternative DNS address as a forwarder on the router, or add static entries to your machines.

### Post by Grenage on 2012-01-18
> **Azyx said:**
> How can I put up a local DNS-server?
> **Azyx said:**
> The problem is that I cannot find that option in my router :(.
When I say locally, I mean on the router.  A quick google reveals many complaints about that router's ability to handle much DNS load, so it's probably not worth bothering.

While I would probably just replace the router, if I could not, I would specify the external DNS servers on each device, then add static entries for the other clients.

PS. If I assaign IP manually, every visitors need to config IP# manually

DNS entries on the clients won't be a problem if you're using DHCP reservations, correct; visitors would also be somewhat limited in using IP addresses.  I suppose the question is, will that many visitors need to access local devices by name?

How do I do that? Can every client be a DNS-server for the local net? Visitors don't need computer-names, but It would not hurt ;). I want to be able to browse my home-network thrue Nautilus, and not need to "connect to server" and put in the IP# for servers.

### Post by Grenage on 2012-01-18
> **Azyx said:**
> I could have problems to find [www.google.com](www.google.com) with no activte torrent and download. But anyway..I have now openDNS.

If you don't have much traffic going through, it sounds like they might just be bad DNS servers.

> **Azyx said:**
> How do I do that? Can every client be a DNS-server for the local net? Visitors don't need computer-names, but It would not hurt ;). I want to be able to browse my home-network thrue Nautilus, and not need to "connect to server" and put in the IP# for servers.

Oh no, the clients would not be DNS servers - just DNS clients.  For example, the config file's nameservers on a PC might be 8.8.8.8 and 8.8.4.4 (google's).  That means that all of their DNS requests would head straight out to the external servers.  If you add entries to the host files on the clients, then it will know where the other clients are, and what they are called

---

Oh no, the clients would not be DNS servers - just DNS clients.  For example, the config file's nameservers on a PC might be 8.8.8.8 and 8.8.4.4 (google's).  That means that all of their DNS requests would head straight out to the external servers.  If you add entries to the host files on the clients, then it will know where the other clients are, and what they are called

How do I add servers to the host files on the clients? Where are they placed? Does I be able to use 'Places-->Network'?

### Post by Azyx on 2012-01-18
I found files named 'hosts' in /etc/ and /etc/avahi/

---

### Post by Grenage on 2012-01-19
I should have explained that.  While there may be a way to add them via a GUI in the current Ubuntu, I have no idea what it is.  To add entries for other machines, it should be:

```
/etc/hosts
```

So you could edit it using:
```
gksu gedit /etc/hosts
```

A format example is:

```
127.0.0.1	localhost
192.168.1.4	bobspc
192.168.1.8	maryspc
```

---

### Post by Azyx on 2012-01-19

What do the file /etc/avahi/hosts do? There you should add .local after computer name.

### Post by Grenage on 2012-01-19
Glad to hear it. :)

As far as I am aware, avahi is for multicast DNS entries, so it's something you probably won't need to worry about.
